data.title: Gauging The Fear Factor: From Volatility Peaks To Equity Returns
data.description:
From Crisis to Recovery: Global and US Equity Returns After VIX Peaks

Stock markets have been rattled by trade war tensions and economic uncertainty driven by US tariff policies. Yet history suggests that equities have usually performed well in the aftermath of peak market volatility.Investors have been coping with acute market swings in recent weeks. Global and US stocks tumbled in the days after US President Donald Trump unveiled his “Liberation Day” tariffs on April 2. Then we saw a dramatic rebound when a 90-day reprieve on most tariffs was announced on April 9, followed by exemptions for electronic equipment such as smartphones and computers. More volatility is likely in the coming weeks while trade policy remains so fluid.Moments like these are understandably tough for investors to stomach. The pain of severe downturns makes it hard to stay the course, even in the most well-designed long-term investment plan. At the same time, withdrawing from equity markets during steep declines risks locking in losses and forfeiting recovery potential.

How Have Stocks Performed After Very Scary Moments?

History can offer a helpful perspective. It may sound counterintuitive, but during the past quarter century, peak market volatility in very serious crises often gave way to powerful equity market returns in the subsequent 12 months (Display). In months when the VIX Index, an index of US equity market volatility, also known as the fear index, reached between 40 and 50, returns for the MSCI World and S&P 500 averaged 37.4% and 34.4%, respectively, over the next 12 months. And when the VIX breached 50, returns for US and global stocks were also very strong over the following year. Emerging-market equities have also done quite well after VIX peaks. On April 8, the VIX hit 52.3, the highest since the pandemic in March 2020.Some caution is warranted in this analysis. Extreme VIX readings are very uncommon; the index only exceeded 40 nine times at month-end during the 24-year period surveyed above. Of course, past performance doesn’t guarantee future results. And today’s macro and market conditions are unprecedented in many ways. The range of outcomes for economic growth and corporate earnings is especially wide and hard to forecast with so much riding on unpredictable policy decisions.That said, past volatility peaks also came at very scary market moments. The global financial crisis of 2008–2009, the euro debt crisis in 2011 and the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 are etched into investors’ collective memories as some of the most terrifying times in modern financial history.

Staying Invested Is a Strategic Imperative

Nobody knows how the trade war will evolve from here. Peak volatility tends to forebode the worst-case scenarios. But if things turn out better than expected, volatility may ultimately subside and better outcomes than feared can be achieved.Since it’s nearly impossible to time market inflection points, we believe that staying invested in equities is a strategic imperative. In our view, portfolios that focus on company fundamentals can target the quality businesses best positioned to weather trade war challenges and thrive over the long term. It’s also important to deploy strategic and tactical risk-management tools that are suited to the current challenges. Diversified allocations based on disciplined investing processes can provide investors with the confidence to stay in the market through uncertain times—and to benefit from the long-term return potential that often materializes when markets shift from fear to hope.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Perfect Storm Is Forming For REIT Outperformance
data.description:
REITs (VNQ) have been in the doghouse for the past several years due to a brutal combination of headwinds from rising interest rates, overbuilt conditions in numerous sectors, and disruptive technology ranging from e-commerce to the rise of the work-from-home movement hammering retail and office real estate. On top of that, growing violence in cities and growing anti-business politics in some traditional office space centers in the United States like New York and San Francisco have further exacerbated headwinds for commercial real estate.

However, today's valuations, macroeconomic factors, and geopolitical factors are all converging to form what could be the perfect storm for REIT outperformance in the coming months and years. As a result, the sector is looking increasingly opportunistic right. While I have largely been very underweight in the REIT sector in recent years in favor of other high-yield sectors like midstream infrastructure (AMLP) and business development companies (BIZD), I am growing increasingly bullish on the REIT sector. In this article where I will detail why, while also highlighting the main risk that until now has kept me from buying the sector hand over fist.

The Bull Case For REITs

One of the biggest reasons to like REITs right now is the fact that many of them are purely domestic focused and thereby are quite sheltered from the ongoing trade war. While some may get hit, especially if they are more concentrated in retail like Simon Property Group (SPG), which sells a lot of clothing and other products that are imported from China, other more service- and experiential-oriented real estate should do just fine. For example, EPR Properties (EPR) has a lot of experiential real estate that should not suffer at all from tariffs. Additionally, there are triple net lease REITs like Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) and Realty Income (O) that also should do quite well in the face of a trade war because they own a lot of properties like restaurants and car washes that also should not suffer much at all from tariffs, at least in the immediate term. Other sectors that should hold up just fine during a trade war are self-storage REITs like Public Storage (PSA) and residential REITs like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) and Camden Properties Trust (CPT).

Another reason to like REITs is that they tend to be more defensive in nature and therefore are positioned to outperform in the event of an economic downturn that may come from the trade war. For example, blue chip healthcare REITs like Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) and triple net lease REITs like NNN REIT (NNN) are positioned to hold up very well in the event of an economic downturn due to the ongoing trade war. This will especially prove to be the case if the Fed cuts rates to prop up the economy and long-term rates also fall due to a slowing economy, as this would likely be a massive tailwind for REITs.

A third reason to really like REITs right now is the valuations that they currently sell for. In many cases, you can currently buy high-quality commercial real estate with pretty strong balance sheets and high yields for pennies on the dollar. While it is true that, at least for now, interest rates remain elevated, over the long term, real estate is much more than just a bond proxy. This is because they are real assets that actually generate growth, both in terms of their cash flows and in their intrinsic value. Additionally, inflation being slightly elevated is a big reason why interest rates remain high today. That means that construction costs are also increasing, especially since the trade war may further compound that effect, which in turn increases replacement costs for existing real estate assets, which should increase their overall value over time. Therefore, whenever you can buy well-run, conservatively financed, and strategically positioned commercial real estate—especially in a diversified portfolio—at a very attractive valuation, if you have the patience to hold it for the long term, it is hard to imagine losing on an investment like that. Meanwhile, the upside could also be significant.

The Bear Case For REITs

While these three reasons to buy REITs today are quite compelling, there is one big reason why I have yet to go all in on REITs—namely, the fact that while long-term interest rates are elevated relative to where they have been in recent history, they can certainly move much higher still. The reasons to be concerned that they may move much higher are twofold. First of all, inflation has remained persistently high, and interest rates could move materially higher in the event that tariffs have an inflationary impact on pricing. This is especially due to the fact that Jerome Powell of the Federal Reserve seems to be in no rush to cut interest rates and seems to want President Trump to reverse course on his tariffs instead before he would consider moving to cut interest rates.

The other big reason why there is risk to higher long-term interest rates is because the global economy does seem to be de-dollarizing to some extent. Things were already headed in that direction in the wake of U.S. sanctions on Russia following its invasion of Ukraine, as countries began to move away from dollars and towards alternative stores of value like gold in an effort to avoid the worst of sanctions in the event that they ran afoul of the United States in the future. But now with the ongoing trade war, many central banks are further accelerating their move away from the U.S. dollar in favor of things like gold due to the U.S.'s increasing willingness to pull economic levers to pressure allies to get more favorable trade agreements.

Additionally, China is making a growing push to try to challenge U.S. global economic hegemony, which is further weighing on the attractiveness of dollars. As a result, the U.S. dollar has continued weakness, and the threat of major treasury holders like China and Japan potentially dumping their treasuries could exert massive further upside pressure on long-term interest rates, which in turn would be quite harmful to REITs, at least in the near term.

Investor Takeaway

With all of that said, how am I approaching the REIT sector today? While I certainly do have exposure and believe that the REIT sector is more likely than not to outperform significantly moving forward, I am still being guarded in how I am approaching it. In particular, I am looking for positions that are as inflation resistant as possible and also have strong balance sheets that will be least impacted by a scenario in which long-term interest rates spike. I also favor companies that are least impacted by the ongoing trade war and can withstand a recession fairly well.

As a result, I especially like companies with strategically positioned defensive residential real estate and have strong balance sheets as well as defensive safe-haven type REITs like farmland (LAND)(FPI) and woodlands REITs (WY). In particular, I like Rayonier (RYN) because it has a very strong balance sheet at the moment after having sold off most of its non-core assets and substantially deleveraging as a result. This makes it today a near-zero net debt way to invest in a diversified portfolio of 100% U.S. woodlands, making it both a potential beneficiary from the trade war as well as a solid choice for safe-haven and inflation hedge that trades for pennies on the dollar and one of the lowest valuations in its history.

These are the sorts of opportunities that we are targeting at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Apple: iPhone And China Expectations Continue To Move Lower
data.description:
According to Visible Alpha consensus, Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) total revenues for FY 2025 have ticked down close to $5 billion from the beginning of last quarter from $412 billion to $407 billion. This $5 billion move is driven by estimates coming down for the China market from $70 billion to $66 billion. The sentiment seems to reflect a view that the issues surrounding tariffs will slow down the pace of iPhone unit sales along with revenue and profit growth in the company's Greater China business, which includes the mainland, Hong Kong and Taiwan markets. Looking further out to FY 2026 and FY 2027, revenues and operating profit for Greater China have moved lower for the company. However, the Greater China sales growth for FY 2026 is still expected to be 10% year-over-year and the operating profit margin has remained at 42%. With costs and currencies currently in flux, could revenue growth and margins be at risk in the region?

In addition to the tariff issues surrounding the China market, iPhone buying continues to be underwhelming in the US, due to fewer users upgrading. Since last January, expected iPhone units declined to 225 million from 236 million for FY 2025. Currently, iPhone sales are expected to generate $201 billion in 2025, down from $213 billion last fall. In addition, full-year iPhone revenue expectations have continued to edge down since early February 2025, likely driven by decreased expectations around the performance of the China market. While the Greater China business segment is exposed to obvious potential challenges from the tariff dynamics, questions also remain around the iPhone supply chain in the near term and what the cost and price impact to the US segment may or may not be.

Expectations for the high-margin Services segment remained stable for FY 2025 at $108 billion. The gross margin for the Services segment is over 70%, significantly higher than the 36% gross margin for Products. Given the large installed base, we are looking forward to what the company says in the Q2 earnings release about growth in Services and the role of Apple Intelligence in FY 2025. More importantly, will the Services segment be immune to the possible impact of tariffs on Apple’s hardware portfolio, especially the iPhone?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Uber: Hitting The Brakes In The Short-Term
data.description:
Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) has established itself as not only the leading ride-hailing app, but also a growing competitor in the food delivery market. The company now has 171+ million monthly active users and continues to invest in its multi-product portfolio.

Uber has also done a tremendous job turning a profit. It was only a few years ago where investors thought pricing would be a race to the bottom and Uber would never be profitable. That is clearly not that case.

However, the company is largely tied to consumer spending trends and even with the early success they have seen to-date with their international expansion, this is a challenging market.

Under the hood, Uber is doing a lot of great things with the company to drive recurring users, more on that below. Nevertheless, with consumer confidence showing early signs of cracks and general travel volumes starting to lighten, Uber may feel the first-hand impact of a slowing economy.

For now, investors may be better off waiting for a more visible spending environment before jumping into the name. With the company expected to report its financial results on May 7th, that clarity is likely to come soon.

Impressive Financial Standing

One thing that Uber has done extraordinarily well is expanding beyond just offering its traditional ride-hailing service. Over the years, Uber has invested into its ecosystem and expanded with Uber Eats, freight transportation, Uber for Business, Uber Health, and Uber One.

With the expanded offering set, this has not only driven higher multi-product usage by monthly consumers, but also higher propensity to spend with Uber. ~34% of monthly consumers now use multiple Uber products, and this has driven 3.4x higher spend than single-product consumers.

Now can Uber keep expanding its services at the same pace seen in recent years? Probably not. But what Uber has successfully demonstrated is their ability to keep the consumer within the Uber ecosystem. This drives significant efficiencies in terms of the cost to acquire customers. In other words, it is much easier to get an existing Uber ride-hailing consumer to try out Uber Eats then it is to get a consumer to start using the Uber platform for the first time.

Autonomous vehicles is another large area Uber continues to invest in. Although management has discussed the significant TAM unlock here, many believe this is much more of a multi-year journey become self-driving vehicles become mainstream.

From a financial standpoint, the company continues to generate strong growth, even at the current scale. During Q4-2024, total trips grew 18% yoy and the number of monthly active platform consumers grew 14% yoy. This led to revenue growing 21% yoy to nearly $12 billion during the quarter, and ending 2024 with nearly $44 billion in revenue.

Additionally, profitability continues to improve, with adjusted EBITDA growing 44% yoy. This resulted in a ~4.2% adjusted EBITDA margin (relative to gross bookings), a continual upward trend.

During February 2024, the company issued targets for 2024-2026, which included mid- to high-teens gross bookings growth and high-30s% to 40% adjusted EBITDA CAGR, implying ongoing margin expansion. Yes these are very positive financial ambitions, however, the macro environment in February 2024 was much different than where we are today.

More Challenging Spending Environment

Since the start of the year, news headlines around a slowing economy have been very prevalent. In more recent weeks, especially since the “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, uncertainties around consumer spending intentions have been questioned.

For starters, the consumer confidence index, as reported by The Conference Board, fell once again in March, reaching the lowest level in 12 years. Data from this chart is only through March 19, and updated consumer confidence data is likely to become available in the coming weeks.

Views of current business conditions weakened to close to neutral. Consumers’ expectations were especially gloomy, with pessimism about future business conditions deepening and confidence about future employment prospects falling to a 12-year low. Meanwhile, consumers’ optimism about future income—which had held up quite strongly in the past few months—largely vanished, suggesting worries about the economy and labor market have started to spread into consumers’ assessments of their personal situations.

Does this inevitably mean that we are entering a recession? Of course not.

Now let us take a look at recent travel trends.

Entering the year, many airlines were bullish about domestic travel activity. However, during March, many airlines started tapering their demand expectations, warning that travel demand is slowing.

During United Airlines’ recent earnings call, management discussed the fluid demand environment, citing the potential risk of a recession further negatively impacting travel trends.

There's a tremendous amount of uncertainty in the economy right now and we've already seen a reduction in demand and correspondingly, in revenue, but we've seen stability at that lower demand level in the last six weeks.

While we've seen stability in demand for the past six weeks, we also recognize that there's a real risk of the US economy going into a recession. If we enter a recession, we are modeling an additional 5-point reduction in total revenue for the remainder of the year on average per quarter.

Although Uber’s business is not 100% correlated with travel trends, domestic consumers are much more likely to need a ride-hailing service or order Uber Eats during their travel.

A slower period of travel and more caution around consumer spending was last seen during COVID in 2020. Granted, this situation will be 100% different than a potential economic slowdown, as traveling was essentially 100% halted. However, Uber’s business would be negatively impacted by these trends.

Valuation

Yes, some of the above risks around potential slowdown in consumer spending and travel slowdown has likely been priced into the stock in some fashion.

The biggest concern is the unknown around how these trends have fared thus far into April and how Uber expects the remainder of the year to play out.

Looking at a revenue multiple for Uber may not be the most appropriate method, thus many investors tend to look at EBITDA multiples.

At face value, the stock’s current multiple does not appear aggressive. However, this is using potentially stale estimates as the demand environment has quickly shifted over the past 6-8 weeks, long after Uber provided its last quarterly update.

Important to note that the stock is only slightly down from the pre-Liberation Day levels, essentially implying that investors do not see incremental headwinds as a result of the tariffs.

I believe the current stock price does not appropriately reflect the heightened risks in recent weeks, thus, remaining on the sidelines until the next quarterly report in a few weeks.

Risks

The biggest risk to Uber would be a slowdown in consumer spending. If the average consumer is less likely to travel or spending on take-out food, this would negatively impact the business.

Competition is also large and growing. Domestic competitors such as Lyft and GrubHub are well-known, and the market has been dominated by only a few players. A slowdown in consumer spending may result in competition changing its pricing tactics in order to gain more market share.

Finally, Uber has been investing a lot into growing its ecosystem, and many of these bets have paid off. However, if the company overextends some of these investments and does not see the financial return, profitability may be at risk.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: 2025 'Top Picks' Top Performers Speak Volumes
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Every year for the last few decades, we’ve produced an annual “Top Picks” report. It contains the best investing ideas for the coming year from a rotating roster of our top expert contributors.

I track them using Yahoo Finance’s portfolio tool... and the MoneyShow Chart of the Day (table, really) speaks volumes about how the market’s character has changed radically. The table shows which recommendations in the 2025 Top Picks Report have performed the best through this week.

The top performer is Avino Silver & Gold Mines Ltd. (ASM, ASM:CA), with a tracked gain of 113%. It’s followed by Heliostar Metals Ltd. (OTCQX:HSTXF, HSTR:CA) at +108%, then United States Antimony Corp. (UAMY), Erdene Resource Development Corp. (OTCPK:ERDCF, ERD:CA), and Agnico Eagle Mines Ltd. (AEM, AEM:CA) with solid double-digit open gains.

What do they all do? Explore for and mine precious, base, and rare earth metals! Among the top 10 performers, only a couple are in any other sector (financials and consumer discretionary). Federal National Mortgage Association (OTCQB:FNMA) is a “special situation,” too - a highly speculative pick the contributor identified as such from the outset.

Clearly, this is a very different kind of market. One where the makeup of winners and losers is a lot different than the Magnificent Seven-dominated market we had several quarters back. Be sure to keep that in mind as a trader or investor.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TNA: Buy For Big Gains On Small Caps
data.description:
Introduction to TNA

Direxion Daily Small Cap Bull 3X Shares (NYSEARCA:TNA) is a triple leveraged ETF that tracks the performance of the Russell 2000 index. It aims to provide 300% of the daily movement of its underlying benchmark. Here are some of the high- level key facts:

  • Expense Ratio: 1.05% (Prospectus)
  • Average Volume: 14M (Prospectus)
  • AUM: $1.51B (Seeking Alpha)

Its top holdings are listed in the table below:

Its top sector weightings are listed here:

When reviewing ETFs, we normally analyze the top holdings to help us understand if the ETF has overall growth opportunities. However, TNA is very diversified with the top holding only accounting for 0.74% of the fund. The top 10 holdings together make up only 4.24%. So instead, we will look at the top sectors within the ETF: Financials, Industrials and Health Care. The top sector (financials) makes up 19.77% of the fund while the top 3 together make up 54.12%. With such high concentration in the top sectors, we can be confident that any movement within those sectors will materially impact the performance of TNA.

Financials: We are bullish on the financial sector as the volatility of the markets currently have already led to many banks reporting strong trading revenues. The latest earnings from JP Morgan and Goldman Sachs have shown that while there is significant turmoil in the markets, many banks are profiting off of increased trading activity to counteract the current downmarket in M&A and IPO activity. Additionally, in this uncertain environment, the Fed has come out and said they have to “wait and see” before reducing interest rates further - this pause in the lowering of rates will continue to help banks’ bottom lines through higher net interest margins. Alternatively, the tariff situation becomes more clear, and the markets begin to run wild again - this inherently helps the financial sector through decreased delinquencies in their consumer divisions and stronger commercial banking and corporate banking activity as deal flow increases. Either way, the financial sector is a safer bet amongst the ongoing market fluctuations, and seeing that this ETF’s largest sector of holdings is financials is a positive sign.

Industrials: There is projected to be a wave of infrastructure spending stemming from the 2021 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law - over $1.2 trillion in fact. In the era of tariffs and uncertainty, this of course could be slowed or reduced by the current administration, but we doubt that will occur. In fact, we believe that many of the smaller contractors in the Russell 2000 will actually benefit from ongoing investment and construction in the infrastructure space. Furthermore, many of these holdings and companies are US based and have US based supply chains - which limit their exposure to tariff risk. In fact, two of the top ten holdings, Applied Industrial Technologies and Beacon Roofing Supply, for example, are both US based and conduct a majority of their business within the US - which should be good for reducing tariff risk. None of these companies will be completely safe from the impact of tariffs as they constantly may change, but we believe that a focus on the industrial sector as a whole will be beneficial for investors in TNA.

Healthcare: We believe that healthcare companies should do well for 2 main reasons outside of the established fact that more and more senior citizens require care every day - primarily, 1) outsized impact of AI on drug development and healthcare efficiency, and 2) protection of the sector during a downturn. For starters, there is significant investment going into healthcare AI that should reduce the amount of time it takes for new drugs to be developed and tested. In terms of streamlining efficiencies, there is an incredible amount of waste that is present in administrative and clinical work streams in the healthcare industry. The leveraging of AI to improve this entire sector should lead to positive financial outcomes for the companies involved in the space. Secondly, healthcare is something that individuals will need no matter how the broader markets or economy is doing. Sure, there will be ups and downs as there are in any investment, but no matter what - people need to get checkups and remain healthy. Since we are in a period where the markets are so uncertain and the macroeconomic environment remains murky, we think that exposure to healthcare is a solid move in a balanced portfolio.

Impacts of Leverage

Since TNA is a leveraged ETF, its movements and potential risk profile are not always very intuitive to understand.

Loss Recoupment

The main takeaway when it comes to loss recoupment is to keep in mind is that if you lose X% on an investment today, it needs to go up by more than X% tomorrow for you to breakeven. In other words, losses have a bigger impact than gains. This is also a non-linear relationship meaning that the gain required on day 2 to breakeven grows faster than the corresponding losses on day one. This is illustrated visually in the chart below.

This relationship is even steeper when working with a leveraged ETF. The chart below compares the day 1 loss vs the day 2 gain required for a non-leveraged investment (blue line which is the same as the above chart) with the day 1 loss for the underlying equity vs the day 2 gain required for breakeven on if you owned a 3x leveraged ETF (red line).

Volatility

Volatility of the underlying instrument also plays a big factor in the investment returns for a 3x leveraged ETF. In short - if 2 stocks both increased by X% over a period of time, but stock A had more negative days along the way, then the 3x leveraged returns on stock B will be higher than the 3x leveraged returns on stock A, sometimes by a significant margin.

As you can see, even though Stock A and Stock B had the same performance for the 5 days, the 3x leveraged versions of each had a non-trivial difference in returns. Due to this phenomenon, it’s generally better to hold leveraged ETFs for shorter periods of time. The longer you hold a leveraged ETF, it is likely that you will experience a higher number of negative days, which will impact your overall returns.

Small Cap Risks

Volatility

By definition, the Russell 2000 index - the underlying index of TNA - consists of small cap companies. More specifically, the smallest 2,000 companies in the Russell 3000 Index. Smaller companies will come with higher risk for a number of reasons including: 1) The companies are more likely to be in earlier stages of growth and are competing against larger incumbents and 2) the companies will have fewer resources to invest in growth or weather an economic “storm”. The risk is higher especially in volatile markets such as the ones we find ourselves in now. See below for a daily returns correlation chart between the Russell 2000 (^RUT) and the CBOE Volatility Index (^VIX)

As you can see, RUT and VIX move in almost the opposite directions with a correlation score of -0.9. A score of -1 would indicate that the two move in exactly the opposite directions. So overall, not only is volatility a major risk when it comes to leveraged ETFs as we discussed above, but also in TNA’s case it is a risk to the underlying index too.

Tariff Uncertainty

The recent tariff wars create a lot of uncertainty in the market. When there is higher uncertainty or an overall bearish trend, investors tend to flock towards stable, more predictable investments and away from riskier investments such as small cap equities. Additionally, small cap companies in general will have less leverage with both suppliers and customers when it comes to pricing so it is likely that they will need to absorb most or all of the additional cost. This could impact margins significantly and is something to keep an eye on as the tariff news continues to come out of the White House, especially during this 90-day pause on implementations.

Conclusion and Recommendations

Considering the loss recoupment and volatility risks associated with leveraged ETFs, our recommendation for investing in TNA is to hold it for short periods of time in order to reduce the impacts of volatility. One strategy for leveraged ETFs such as TNA is to play catalysts - whether positive or negative. There is significant volatility that is happening in the current market and as a result, there are opportunities to play the swings more so than in a calmer period.

Consider the difference in performance of TNA versus its underlying benchmark Year-to-Date. The Russell 2000 is down ~16.5% while TNA has cratered nearly ~47.5%. While this is alarming, it shows that the volatility can be wielded as a weapon to outsize gains via short term holds and sells of TNA.

Overall, we are bullish on the long-term growth prospects of the Russell 2000 but remain cautious - especially on the leveraged TNA - given the current state of the markets. Investors should be very careful while holding this name for short term periods.

data.title: KREF.PR.A: High Risk, Yet Significant Upside Potential
data.description:
KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. 6.5% SER A PFD (NYSE:KREF.PR.A) is in focus today as we dial in on another discounted preferred stock. KKR Real Estate Finance's Series A preferred shares, aka KREF Series A traded at a discount of over 27% upon authoring this article. The stock's discount comes at little surprise given the changing systematic environment. More specifically, KREF Series A peaked at $23.30 in September last year; however, factors such as interest rate cuts in late 2024 and geopolitical instability in 2025 detracted value from Series A.

Some might argue that Series A is in value trap territory. We acknowledge the possibility. Yet, we'd like to highlight the possibility that an outlying opportunity might be in store for risk-tolerant investors.

Here's why we think it's a good idea to start buying the dip on KREF Series A.

Series A's Optics

KREF's Series A is attractively priced for a company with a credit rating of B+. Though current yields of near 9% exist, you'd often be looking at distressed situations or preferred shares that have recently priced call risk. Instead, Series A has yet to reach its call date and as show later in the article, KREF maintains commendable fundamentals.

Metric Value
Market Price 16/04/'25 $18.12
Liquidation Value $25
Call Date 4/16/2026
Annualised Coupon 1.625
Current Yield 8.97%
Initial Yield 6.50%

Source: Preferred Stock Channel

KREF Series A reached a peak in September 2024. Following that, a pivot in U.S. interest rates likely devalued the stock on the basis of spread widening (between Series A's coupon and the 10-Y treasury yield). Additionally, some investors might've pre-positioned for call risk under the assumption that interest rates would fall linearly into mid-2026.

Core Argument

The Opportunity

I'm not going to say that KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc.'s point-in-time fundamentals are splendid. In fact, a reasonable argument exists that U.S. economic growth outlook is slowing. Moreover, credit risk has exacerbated, reflected by the 9.2% default rate in U.S. corporate debt, a figure in excess of the levels experienced during COVID-19.

Despite the shaky credit outlook, we believe Series A presents a solid deep value opportunity. Sure, systematic risk is evident. However, KREF Series A isn't in the same category as a moderately undervalued common stock without dividend obligations; instead, KREF Series A is at a deeply discounted preferred share with cumulative and fixed-rate dividend obligations. Therefore, we argue that KKR Real Estate Finance Trust merely has to maintain reasonable solvency and liquidity metrics for its Series A shares to avoid restructuring or dividend suspension, which, in turn would allow investors to benefit from scintillating current yields while awaiting for the instrument to revert to its liquidation value.

Key Fundamentals Drivers

As things stand, KREF is a senior loans pure play. Key features include: A multi-family portfolio with 92% underlying occupancy (47% of total loan portfolio), 99% floating-rate exposure, and an office portfolio spanning 90% Class A units. Amalgamated, we consider these underlying variables solid and capable of defending against interim economic pressures. Sure, KREF is a cyclical company; however, we see cyclicality being bound to its common shares more than its preferred shares, especially as Series A has already experienced a telling drawdown.

A time series view shows that KREF has remained profitable in all but one of its last eleven fiscal years. This affirms the argument that KREF will likely maintain its preferred share obligations throughout a trying time for the economy. Moreover, KREF's liability structure seems intact, allowing it to cushion again asset-level headwinds. For example, KREF has a total leverage ratio of 3.6x and $3.1 billion in undrawn liquidity capacity. We consider these numbers rather docile and believe solvency won't be an issue unless disaster strikes.

Risk Factors

Despite viewing KREF Series A as a buy-the-drawdown opportunity, we recognise the plethora of emerging risks. As previously mentioned, the credit cycle is doubtful, which isn't ideal with option-adjusted spreads having spiked back into rangebound territory (from a compressed level). We think additional high-yield OAS widening is possible given that High-Yield OAS remains below its long-run average and considering the macroeconomic headwinds. In our view, sub investment grade credit investors could sell aggressively until OAS values peak out above the 6% - 7.5% level.

In essence, rising credit risk, especially via spreads, could result in KREF Series A falling even further via systematic pressure.

Furthermore, market liquidity has dried up, echoed by the receding S&P 500 liquidity marker. Lower market liquidity can translate into preferred shares, creating market impact and asymmetrical downside risk.

Conclusion

KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. 6.5% SER A PFD seems like a risky bet given the depth is emerging risks within the credit cycle. However, KREF Series A has dipped into a 27%+ discount-to-liquidation after shedding from its peak in September 2024, presenting a potential buy-the-drawdown scenario. We think it's an excellent time to think long term and tap into KREF Series A's near 9% current yield while waiting for it to revert to its liquidation value, especially as solvency and liquidity risks remain docile.

data.title: Rocket Lab: A $515 Million Problem
data.description:
I perfectly remember when Rocket Lab USA, Inc. (RKLB) popped up in my momentum screener back in Q4 last year. I was considering betting on this stock for a couple of weeks in a row, but I ended up jumping on the Quantum wave instead (I am glad I did).

Fast forward to today, and this stock still hasn’t made its way into my portfolio. In fact, I initiate my coverage with a sell rating.

Believe it or not, my rating has nothing to do with its sky high valuation ratios. I couldn't care less of valuation ratios for a company that doesn't have any direct, pure-play competitors (that trade publicly) to compare them to. Furthermore, the company is growing revenues in the double and even triple digits, as noted in Q4 2024.

I hint in this introduction that my sell rating is mainly based on the risk related to a contract worth half a billion dollars with the SDA. On top of that, around 80% of Rocket Lab's business is tied (directly and indirectly) to the defense sector, which adds another layer of risk considering Pete's 8% cost cutting plan.

Overall, I don't see enough upside to justify holding this stock in the near term. Maybe, once the Neutron rocket is fully operational and commercially available, I may change my mind.

That said, I don't think Rocket Lab is suitable for a short play either.

SDA's Tranche 2 Contract At Risk

Here is a tail risk that would keep me up at night if I owned this stock. According to some reports, the U.S. Air Force may reconsider future satellite projects from the Space Development Agency (SDA).

As you may know, the SDA is building a network of satellites for military purposes. The project is divided into the following tranches:

  • Tranche 0: 28 sats (19 transport, 8 tracking, 1 ground testbed) launched in 2023.
  • Tranche 1: 126 transport satellites, set to launch in summer 2025 (after some delays). Orbit testing is planned to be complete by mid-2026, and the delivery of initial warfighting capability by early 2027. Contractors in this tranche include Northrop Grumman, Lockheed Martin, York Space Systems, and L3Harris.
  • Tranche 2: 216 sats (100 Alpha sats by York Space Systems and Northrop Grumman, 90 Beta by Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and Rocket Lab, and 20 Gamma by York Space Systems and Tyvak Nano-Satellite Systems) with a launch timeline starting in 2026. RocketLab was awarded back in 2024 a $515 million contract to build 18 Beta variant sats.
  • Tranche 3: ~200 sats, launching around 2028. The SDA has recently opened the contest for 54 missile tracking sats.

Here is the thing. Trump recently signed an executive order, mandating the DOD to review military projects that are behind schedule and/or over budget. It turns out that SDA's satellite program may be at risk, as recently noted by Citi analysts.

In my view, the biggest risk for the four contractors involved in Tranche 2 (RocketLab is one of them) is SpaceX and its Starlink constellation of over 7,194 satellites. Yes, I agree with every bull on RocketLab that contract termination fees could deter the US Air Force (they are sitting on top of the SDA) from cancelling the Tranche 2 and 3 programs. However, no one knows the exact clauses of the $515 million contract. If the cost savings of using the Starlink constellation surpass the costs of early termination contract penalties, we could be in for an unpleasant surprise this year.

Considering Pete Hegseth's $50 billion cost cutting target for FY 2026 (which, by the way, extends to the next 5 years, with an 8% reduction target each year), I believe that any program that is not delivering results may be at high risk. As noted above, SDA's Tranche 1 program was already delayed. Additionally, the Tranche 0 project has been criticized by the Government Accountability Office (GAO) for not meeting certain requirements.

Other risks that would keep me up at night include a stagnation in backlog growth, which increased just 2% last year, sitting at $1.07B. Do bear in mind that a good part of this backlog represents part of the $515 million contract for the 18 Beta variant sats within SDA's Tranche 2 program.

Moving on, looking at the timeline for the first launch of the Neutron rocket, it seems that management has delayed the first launch from mid 2025 to H2 2025. What I found disappointing during the Q&A is the fact that revenue from this model is likely not expected this year. The first launch will be an R&D launch.

So, when you think about neutron costs, obviously this first test launch is an R&D launch. There won't be any revenue associated with that launch. And so as you look forward, of course, there's going to be, as we get some production efficiencies as you move from the first tail to the second tail to the third tail, that's always important.

Also, from the Q&A, I was able to find the company's dependency on government contracts.

Yeah, it might even be higher than that. I think it's probably closer to north of 80% would be where the end, I'd say consumption of the data coming off the things that we launch get consumed in one way or other by a government, whether it's US government or other governments.

Please bear in mind that in the prepared remarks, CFO Adam Spice said the following.

At first glance, our backlog has roughly a 50-50 split between government and commercial.

It turns out that some of the commercial programs are serving defense and national security customers. Therefore, I see a company highly exposed to the defense sector (particularly in the US), which is not something I find attractive considering the current cost-cutting measures that I discussed above.

Why I Wouldn't Short This Stock

Here is why I believe RocketLab is not suitable for a short play.

Let's start with price action, and then I'll move to fundamentals and valuation.

After the sharp selloff prior to Q4 2024 earnings, which cut nearly 50% off the stock’s value since its peak in January, the share price is now holding steady after testing a support level in the mid-$10 range. Over the past month, the stock has outperformed the S&P 500, which is a positive sign considering the uncertainties in the US trade policy.

From an operational perspective, the revenue from launching a vehicle finally topped the costs in 2024.

This could reduce the cash burn rate of the company in the long term, especially once the Neutron is operational. In fact, according to the earnings call, this rocket is designed to be reused at least 20 times. This could improve gross margins in the launch services segment, which were 27.6% in FY 2024.

However, the company expects cash burn in Q1 2025 to double, mainly due "neutron-related investments as well as long-lead procurement items for our SDA program and a lack of significant contractual milestone payments receivable across our MDA Global Star and SDA programs in the quarter", according to management during the latest earnings call.

Considering the $419 million in cash & ST investments, and the recent prospectus for the offer and sale of up to $500M of common stock, I am not too concerned with liquidity at the moment.

From a growth perspective (leaving aside profitability), the company reported 5 consecutive quarters of (at least) double digit revenue growth, with the last quarter seeing a 120% yoy increase. Interestingly enough, this growth is mainly driven by the systems segment and not by launch services.

Additionally, Rocket Lab recently announced that it will be involved in two major defense programs backed by the US (EWAAC program) and UK (HTCDF program) governments. The overall value of the US contract is $46 billion, running until 2031, while the UK contract is worth around $1.3 billion. Just to be clear, Rocket Lab is now eligible to bid for these projects, but they haven't been granted a specific dollar amount yet.

Finally, from a valuation perspective, it's hard to run a solid analysis because Rocket Lab is the only pure play space launch company that's publicly traded. Yes, its 37 P/B ratio looks absurdly high when compared to the median of the industrial sector, and its 24 P/S ratio is equally alarming, however, one cannot use these arguments in favor of a bear case. If SpaceX, Blue Origin, or Firefly were trading publicly, then a valuation analysis would make sense, as one would be able to compare these ratios on an apples-to-apples basis.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I believe Rocket Lab carries too much contract risk tied to the US defense budget. I believe that the $515 million deal could be at risk due to Pete Hegseth's $50 billion cost cutting target for FY 2026. Furthermore, I see delays in the Tranche 1 program and criticisms by the Government Accountability Office in the Tranche 0 program.

Overall, the company’s heavy reliance on government contracts (~80% of total revenue when accounting for commercial customers serving defense projects) makes it especially vulnerable to the cost cutting in this sector, and the recent executive order to cut 8% defense spending each year over the next 5 years, adds more pressure.

I rate Rocket Lab as a Sell, although I wouldn't short it as the company is showing strong growth, and the upcoming Neutron rocket could improve margins next year, lifting investor sentiment.

data.title: Adriatic Metals Wants To Join The World's Largest Silver Miners Group (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
A “Buy” Rating for Adriatic Metals PLC

This article changes the rating of US-listed shares of Adriatic Metals PLC (OTCPK:ADTLF) from a previous sell recommendation to a buy recommendation. Adriatic Metals is a multi-metallic exploration and development company headquartered in Cheltenham, UK, whose flagship project is the 100%-owned Vareš silver project, covering an area of ​​approximately 50 square kilometres in Bosnia and Herzegovina.

The Sprott Mining Risk Heat Map shows a medium risk for exploration and mining activities in Bosnia and Herzegovina.

The rating change is justified by the Vareš ramp-up to commercial production expected in Q1 2025, which will enable 625 to 675 kt of ore to be milled and 12,000 to 13,000 k oz AgEq to be produced in 2025. Plus, the company is raising capital to finance the expansion of the plant capacity from 0.8 Mtpa (nameplate production) to 1.3 million Mtpa in a very favourable silver price environment, to take advantage of as production is expressed in silver equivalent ounces.

Since Our Previous Sell Rating, This Is How ADTLF Stock Has Performed

Since our last article on January 29, 2023, ADMLF's share price has risen by 22.13%, contrary to our previous Sell recommendation, compared to 32% for the S&P 500, although we must emphasise that we did not recommend selling the positions completely. We recognised the potential of the Vareš silver project and were observing the prospect of a rising silver price. Rather, we considered it appropriate to reduce the positions in anticipation of a sharp price decline, which we predicted was very likely given the volatility of the shares. In fact, ADMLF's share price fell significantly on February 21, 2024, and even bottomed out in the first half of August 2024. The first significant lower share price occurred after hopes for a Federal Reserve interest rate cut had tempered, which weighed on the silver price and, by virtue of a positive correlation, also on the ADMLF share price. Silver does not generate income, and thus, the appetite for silver diminishes if interest rates tend to stay higher in the long term. The bottom in stock prices instead occurred as precious metals were caught in a broader market plunge amid fears of an impending economic recession. Had investors considered these declines and subsequently perhaps managed to reposition themselves somewhat, likely, they would have enjoyed even better results.

Vareš Silver Project In Bosnia And Herzegovina: Company Makes Progress

The Vareš Silver Project is located 35 km north of Sarajevo in Bosnia and Herzegovina, in an area historically known for metal production, where easy access to key infrastructure is mitigating the moderate risk depicted in the Sprott Mining Risk Heat Map mentioned above.

The Vareš silver project was expected to reach commercial production last quarter and has already produced 934,000 ounces of silver equivalent (“AgEq”) in the fourth quarter and a total of 1,335,000 ounces of AgEq for the full year 2024.

Expansion Of The Facility: Completion By 2027

The Rupice and Rupice NW deposits have mineral resources along strike and at depth that could secure production activities for nearly 10 years. The company is therefore optimistic about the mill expansion project. With appropriate financing, capacity could reach 1.3 million tonnes per year by 2027, from a maximum capacity currently set at 0.8 million tonnes per year.

Adriatic Metals Raises Money For The Factory Expansion Projects

The company estimates that capital expenditures of US$25 million are required to increase throughput to 1.3 million tonnes per year. Financing will be provided by the offering of new shares on the Australian Stock Exchange as approved by the regulatory system for wished total of A$80.0 million (approximately US$50 million).

The company also had cash on hand: as of February 17, 2025, this was A$73.6 million (approximately US$46 million), including the US$25 million concentrate prepayment received from physical metal trader Trafigura.

The financial position as of February 17, 2025, was weighed down by a first-quarter debt repayment to the lender, “Orion”, of US$19 million, which was postponed from December 31, 2024, to March 31, 2025.

Adriatic Metals expects annual production of 20 million ounces AgEq by 2027, which would make Vares Silver Operation one of the largest primary silver producers in the world, as compared to visualcapitalist.com's “Ranked: Top Silver Producing Companies of 2023.”

Rising Production, The World's Lowest-Cost Primary Silver Mine And Positive Silver Price Dynamics Create The Conditions For Strong Cash Flow Generation

Cash flow and profitability are well-positioned to function as a strong upward catalyst for the share price, as the 3 key inputs are all oriented in their favour

First: Robust production and on track to achieve the 20 million ounces AgEq milestone by 2027, representing growth of over 50% from a full-year 2025 guidance of 12,000-13,000 k oz AgEq

Second: Vares is the“lowest cost primary silver mine globally,” according to Adriatic Metals' chart below, based on a comparison of 2025 Adriatic production guidance and primary silver mines (on a 100% basis) with expected 2025 production extrapolated from S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Third: Positive Commodities Price Dynamics

Vares will also produce zinc and gold, so its profitability also depends on the future price of these commodities. However, since the company primarily produces silver and production is actually expressed in ounces of silver equivalent, creating a strong connection between Adriatic Metals and the commodity silver among market participants, silver will have the greatest influence on the sentiment surrounding Adriatic Metals on the stock markets.

Silver Spot Price (XAGUSD:CUR) traded at US$32.91/oz, increased 13.97% YTD and increased 16.82% over the past 12 months, as of this writing.

Silver is not only a safe haven like its lustrous cousin gold, as it is not for nothing that it is referred to as the “poor man’s gold,” but Silver is also benefiting from robust industrial demand, leading to upward pressures on prices through continued silver market deficit.

The Silver Institute wrote on April 16, 2025:

“ Overall, global silver demand exceeded silver supply for the fourth consecutive year, resulting in a structural market deficit of 148.9 Moz in 2024. Notably, during 2021-2024, the combined deficit reached 678 Moz, equivalent to 10 months of global mine supply in 2024. ”

This is because the 1.7 per cent year-on-year growth in global silver supply to 1,015.1 million ounces in 2024 once again failed to keep pace with global silver demand of 1,164.1 million ounces. Despite a 2.9% year-on-year decline, global demand for silver remained substantial due to a new record high in industrial silver demand.

Positive factors for industrial demand were observed in the areas of green power grid infrastructure, deployment of electric vehicles and solar panels, and the consumer electronics sector, as people were attracted by the hype surrounding AI software.

Looking ahead to 2025, the Silver Institute predicts that

“the silver market is anticipated to remain in a deficit, but this gap will be a four-year low of 117.6 Moz.”

Earlier this month, Saxo Bank stated, according to Seeking Alpha, that “silver may reach $44 per ounce” by 2025, which is a 34 per cent soar from the current spot price.

Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) traded at US$$3,328.70/oz, increased 26.81% YTD and increased 39.95% over the past 12 months, as of this writing.

Seeking Alpha writes:

“ Gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) hit another record high on Wednesday as concerns over economic growth, due to uncertainty surrounding U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs, resulted in market volatility.

Gold, often considered a safe-haven investment because it typically acts as a hedge in times of political and financial uncertainty, has risen over 25% so far this year, with investors eyeing more gains in the coming days. ”

ANZ gold price forecast:

“ "We believe risk-off purchases for gold are yet to pick up," ANZ said, as it increased its year-end gold price forecast to $3,600 per ounce and six-month forecast to $3,500.”, according to Seeking Alpha.

Goldman Sachs gold price forecast:

“  Goldman Sachs has increased its end-2025 gold price forecast to $3,700 per ounce from $3,300, its third such hike this year, as trade tensions escalate.

[…]

 The Bank anticipates stronger-than-expected central bank demand and ETF inflows.

 The investment bank had in March hiked its 2025 gold price target to $3,300/oz.

 Goldman Sachs warned that in an extreme risk case, gold could reach as high as $4,500/oz by end-2025, according to reports, according to Seeking Alpha.

UBS gold price forecast:

“UBS on Friday lifted its gold (XAUUSD:CUR) forecast to $3,500/oz as the safe-haven metal clinched another record high amid heightened global trade tensions.

The Bank also raised its upside and downside targets by $300/oz to be $3,800/oz and $3,200/ oz, respectively.

[...]

UBS reiterated its 'attractive preference' for the metal and stays long in global and Asia asset strategies.

UBS said this rally is also due to how trade-related uncertainties are impacting trust in the US dollar and US Treasuries, as it expects additional demand from central banks, institutions, and private investors following current events.

Futhermore, "positioning remains below the peaks observed during gold’s previous multi-year bull run after the global financial crisis," analysts said.”, according to Seeking Alpha.

Zinc Futures (LMZSDS03:COM) are trading at $2,589.15/tonne, down 13.41% YTD and down 6.07% over the past 12 months, as it has been pressured by the crisis in the zinc-hungry real estate development industry in China and the slowdown in the manufacturing sector in the West and especially Germany, which has been struggling with a recession for the third year now.

The price is still seen as suffering by analysts at Trading Economics despite China's promises to stimulate its economy. Zinc is expected to trade at $2,603.21/MT by the end of this quarter, but looking ahead, it is estimated at $2,447.77 in 12 months (see the “Forecast” section of this Trading Economics webpage).

Stock Price: Shears Are Too High Relative To The Risks. Better to Add From Dips

At the time of writing, Adriatic Metals PLC's US-listed shares were trading under the symbol ADTLF at $2.84 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $937.73 million.

The shares are near the peak of the price cycle, trading above the MA Ribbon and the midpoint of $2.535 per share of the 52-week range of $1.77/share to $3.30 per share. The 14-day RSI suggests further upside potential, but the scope for this is less than for a downside move. A temporary price pullback could occur, reflecting heightened volatility due to uncertainty surrounding President Trump's increased tariffs and retaliatory measures by other countries. This stock has a Buy rating, but we recommend only buying it after a significant price decline to hedge the risks associated with Adriatic Metals in your portfolio:

The first risk is that the Vares project in Bosnia has just reached commercial production and now needs to prove with facts that it offers the world's lowest cost per ounce of silver equivalent production. What appears in documents and presentations now needs to be substantiated with facts after the mine has successfully been commissioned. If the company delivers an AISC/oz that falls short of expectations, this could have negative consequences for the share price.

The second risk factor, in our opinion, is that Adriatic Metals PLC (symbol “ADTLF”) shares are characterised by extremely low daily trading volume: the average volume (3 months) was 1,915 out of a total of 345.30 million shares outstanding. If an investor holds too many ADTLF shares and suddenly needs to sell a certain number of shares quickly, such low daily trading volume could make it difficult to achieve the desired return or, in the worst case, lead to losses.

The third risk factor is contained in this statement from The Silver Institute:

“ As outlined in World Silver Survey 2025, the impact of US tariffs will be a key risk to silver demand this year. An extended period of elevated tariffs, or a further escalation of global trade wars, could lead to significant supply chain disruptions and sharply lower global GDP growth. These will weigh on industrial, jewelry, and silverware demand, though physical investment could benefit from rising safe-haven purchases. ”

Conclusion

Adriatic Metals PLC has been upgraded to Buy from Sell as the company has achieved commercial production at its Vares project in Bosnia. This will generate strong cash flow and high profitability.

The company is financing an expansion project to increase annual silver equivalent production by 55% to 20,000 ounces by 2027. The Vares project will be one of the largest silver mines in the world, implying very low average operating costs per ounce (the “AISC,” namely).

The silver price provides a very favourable environment for successful operations, but gold also has what it needs to extend its amazing uptrend.

The stock is highly priced by the market. While there is still room for further upside, the magnitude is not as if the stock is suddenly moving lower amid volatility caused by concerns about economic growth from the global trade war. Investors would be wise to buy on any price true dips to also protect themselves from some of the risks mentioned in the analysis.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CTO Realty: Both The Ordinary And Series A Preferred Shares Flash Value
data.description:
CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (NYSE:CTO) is one of our long-time favourites. This article reiterates our positive outlook while conveying a detailed analysis of the REIT's common and series A preferred shares. We last covered CTO Realty in January, stating it would likely benefit from relative advantages. Though CTO hasn't delivered positive returns to date, we reiterate our stance in the belief that CTO REIT might be a ballast to those seeking sustained exposure to the capital markets regardless of the uncertain economic environment.

Herewith are our latest thoughts.

Fundamental Review

Asset Level

Same Property Performance

CTO Realty achieved scale in fiscal 2024, illustrated by its 26% growth in total square feet, which was largely achieved by acquisitions and lease renewals. Additionally, CTO's stockholder equity and long-term debt values increased by roughly 33.94% and 4.77% apiece in 2024, resulting in a 33% year-over-year increase in enterprise value. Although we think an occupancy of 90.3% is relatively mild, we see other benefits in CTO Realty; a discussion about such benefits and potential tradeoffs succeeds in the following diagram.

As discussed in some of our previous CTO reports, the REIT has diversified sector exposure, allowing it to smooth its revenue. The ongoing global trade war might result in opposing outcomes for different sectors of the economy, and therefore we find CTO's diversification compelling. Moreover, the REIT has noteworthy exposure to defensive industries, namely health care, off-price retail, grocery stores, and casual dining, adding a secondary layer to its smoothed earnings potential.

A glance at CTO's tenant base transmits a few benefits. For instance, CTO utilises a core anchor approach, allowing it pricing advantages over smaller tenants. In addition, CTO's tenant-specific exposure seems relatively diversified as its top 20 tenants span merely 20% of the portfolio.

On the other side of the playing field, we identified a few systematic risks. More specifically, only 21% of CTO's tenant base is investment grade-rated, suggesting an economic downturn might sever the REIT's counter-party risk.

Lease rollovers were a strong driver for CTO in 2024, communicated by its comparable leasing spread of 23%. In management's own words: "During the quarter ended December 31, 2024, the Company signed 16 leases totaling 67,788 square feet. On a comparable basis, which excludes vacancy existing at the time of acquisition, CTO signed 10 leases totaling 52,134 square feet at an average cash base rent of $22.70 per square foot compared to a previous average cash base rent of $21.06 per square foot, representing 7.8% comparable growth."

Higher spreads illustrate pricing power. While we expect spreads to remain strong, a lower U.S. inflation rate might reduce mark-to-market rates on new and CPI-linked leases in 2025, which is a factor worth considering, especially given the plethora of macroeconomic headwinds that have emerged.

Acquisitions

CTO acquired aggressively in 2024, allocating $226.7 million in capital at a blended cap rate of 8.2%. Commercial property values declined in early-to-mid 2024, allowing big buyers such as CTO to capitalise by entering deals at elevated capitalisation rates.

CTO's salient fiscal 2024 purchases included Lake Brandon Village, Millenia Crossing, Carolina Pavilion, Marketplace at Seminole, and Granada Plaza. The spree was followed by a $79.8 million acquisition of a 559,000 square-foot lifestyle centre in Atlanta (sealed in February), boosting CTO's portfolio size by 12%.

Liabilities

An overview of CTO's capital structure illustrates interesting talking points. At face value, the REIT's net debt/EBITDA ratio of 6.33x seems slightly high, particularly if compared to the U.S. diversified REIT average of 5.97x. However, on the plus side, it has to be considered that CTO has $213 million in revolving credit within its debt structure and $222 million in liquidity.

An ageing schedule is presented below, with a discussion following suit.

We don't think CTO will raise a large sum of debt in the near future, largely due to its inflated net debt/EBITDA ratio and secure overnight funding rates being pent-up at approximately 4.36%. Moreover, U.S. credit risk has increased since the turn of the year, posing challenges to those seeking negative or tight-spread issuance.

Having said that, the REIT implemented an at-the-money share issuance in 2024, raising $165.2 million in equity. CTO can reposition its gearing through ATMs and bootstrapping, eventually allowing it to issue large sums of affordable debt at a later stage.

In essence, we think CTO's funding balance sheet will slow in the near term.

PINE

A factor that's often overlooked is CTO REIT's 14.8% ownership of Alpine REIT (PINE). PINE REIT is a smaller retail/diversified REIT with immense yield potential, echoed by its implied capitalisation rate of 8.1%. We recently covered PINE REIT, assigning a positive outlook on the basis of its strong relative attributes. In our view, PINE REIT could act as a valuable pocket of growth for years to come, especially if CTO decides to up its stake.

Ordinary Shares

Valuation

Absolute Valuation

The direct capitalisation method of valuation provides a fair value of approximately $28.26 per share, substantially higher than CTO REIT's April 15 pre-market value of $17.90.

Metric Value
Same Property NOI FY24 $60,411,000
Same Property NOI Growth FY25* $61,015,110
Sector Cap Rates H2 '24 5% - 9.1%
Cap Rate Utilised 8.5%
Shares Outstanding 25,401,176
Latest Market Value Per Share $17.90
Implied Fair Value Per Share $28.26
Our Price Target $21.00

Source: Author's Work. Data: CTO Realty

Although we think CTO has value, the capitalisation method fails to account for dividend distributions, meaning its valuations can be overstated. Nevertheless, the model has application in modern-day REIT analysis when used as a guideline. Practically speaking, we think CTO's current fundamentals warrant a price target of approximately $21.00 per share, a handle the REIT last breached in September 2024. The remainder of the capitalisation rate' method's value can likely be attributed to 1) dividend returns, and 2) an illiquidity premium.

Aside: Illiquidity premiums are often embedded in real estate valuation models. However, publicly traded REITs rarely capture the premium. Thus, we discounted the value out of the model instead of assuming it will be achieved - here's a link explaining the illiquidity premium and its shortcomings.

Moving along, here's a description of the model's key inputs.

  • Cap Rate: CTO REIT traded with an implied cap rate of 7.75% in the latter stages of 2024. However, we think pressured commercial real estate values paired with macroeconomic headwinds warrant higher capitalisation rates. Therefore, we boosted the model's cap rate to 8.5% by incorporating sector-relative data, sampled by CRED iQ in Q4 2024.

  • NOI: CTO expects 1% in the same property net operating income growth for 2025. We adjusted 2024's NOI to a 2025 value, allowing us to model the REIT's perpetual value.
  • Shares Outstanding: We used 2024's diluted shares outstanding. Kindly note that the value will likely change as time passes.

Valuation Multiples

The REIT's funds price-to-funds from operations and price-to-adjusted funds from operation ratios are below 10x. We like these values. For context, Realty Income (O) has P/FFO and P/AFFO ratios of 14.18x and 13.61x. Another comparison would be Simon Property Group, which currently hosts P/FFO and P/AFFO ratios of 11.51x and 12.61x.

To Reiterate: With all else being equal, Lower P/FFO and P/AFFO values suggest potential relative value.

We think CTO has relative value, but it's worth highlighting a few risks regarding P/FFO and P/AFFO ratios. As illustrated in the following diagram, CTO realised disposition gains in Q4, which got backed out of FFO due to asset sales being non-cash costs (income). In addition, straight-line rent adjustments were amended from the AFFO while amortisation was added back.

What am I trying to get at here? FFO and AFFO figures can be misunderstood due to significant non-cash adjustments, making an isolated view of the ratios redundant. It's important to consider AFFO and FFO within context. That said, we remain positive about CTO's FFO and AFFO ratios.

An Isolated View Of Common Dividends

CTO Realty's ordinary shares possess a five-year average yield on cost of 12.6% and a forward yield of 8.45%. We think these yields are immaculate, especially if combined with CTO REIT's valuation outlook. For context, CTO REIT's forward yield exceeds the U.S. 10-year yield by over 400 basis points, illustrating its excess return possibilities.

Preferred Shares (Series A)

Yield & Optionality

CTO Realty Growth, Inc. 6.375% SER A PFD (CTO.PR.A), or CTO's Series A for short, is a cumulative fixed-rate preferred share tranche. Some might disagree, but we prefer cumulative fixed-rate preferred shares as they offer 'dividend guarantees' at known amounts.

To our knowledge, CTO can suspend dividends during challenging financial periods. However, investors will likely be made whole in the long run. Furthermore, a tactical debt restructuring can occur, whereby CTO offers less than liquidation value. However, we see the probability of such an event occurring being infinitesimal for now.

Metric Value
Current Yield 7.86%
Original Coupon 6.375%
Liquidation Value $25.00
Call Date July 6, 2026

Source: Preferred Stock Channel - Data Captured On April 15, 2025

As for other features, Series A has yet to approach its call date, and call risk likely remains low given the elevated interest rate environment. Furthermore, the share traded at a discount of over 20% upon authoring this article, bolstering its current yield to around 7.86%. For context, Series A's current yield is about 150 basis points higher than the Invesco Preferred ETF's (PGX) dividend yield.

GGM Valuation & Practical Implications

The Gordon's Growth method of valuation provides valuable insight into a stable company's perpetual value. The method is commonly used for ordinary shares but can be adjusted to fit a preferred share valuation model by releasing the sustainable growth rate - it's assumed that preferred shares don't share in growth like ordinary shares do.

The GGM for ordinary shares is shown below. Additionally, here's a link explaining how the model can be adjusted for preferred shares.

The GGM suggests that CTO Realty's Series A has a fair value of around $25.71, which is above its liquidation value of 25 per share. An explanation of the model's inputs follows the next diagram.

Metric Value
CAPM 9.2%
Growth* 3%
Market-Inferred Preferred Stock Cost of Capital 6.2%
Annualised Dividend Per Share $1.59376
Market Price (15/04/2025) $20.38
Implied Fair Value Per Share $25.71

Sources: Author's Work. Data: Value.IO, Preferred Stock Channel - Data Captured On April 15, 2025

Herewith is an explanation of the model's key inputs.

  • CAPM & Growth: A range of CAPM and sustainable growth rates are provided by Value.IO. In an attempt to remain conservative, we selected the worst-case scenario values, including CAPM of 9.2% and a perpetual growth rate of 3%.
  • Annual Dividend: The annual dividend was drawn from Preferred Stock Channel.

Will Series A reach its theoretical fair value?

Fixed-income investors would know that preferred shares have a host of variables that influence their returns. As previously mentioned, we think Series A's call risk remains relatively low. Having said that, spread risk could impair Series A's market value if the treasury yield curve inverts during a pre-recession scenario.

An Aside: Spread risk refers to the difference between the coupon and the 10-y treasury yield.

In essence, if you look at the optics, it's evident that Series A has noteworthy market-based risks. Still, we think its current yield is too good to pass up.

Conclusion

Our analysis deems CTO Realty a buy. Key metrics suggest that CTO's Series A preferred shares and common stock hold value, which could be unlocked if CTO's portfolio-level metrics remain intact. Sure, short-term headwinds are worrisome but we think CTO is a long-term play with tremendous idiosyncratic advantages and untapped value.

Consensus: Bullish
